questions.question: Explain the key differences between Traditional and Modern Project Management approaches.
keyPoints: Traditional follows sequential/linear phases while modern follows iterative approach
Traditional requires fixed requirements upfront; modern allows evolving requirements
Traditional has limited customer involvement; modern emphasizes continuous collaboration
Traditional focuses on documentation; modern on working software

questions.question: List and explain the four values of the Agile Manifesto.
keyPoints: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
Working software over comprehensive documentation
Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
Responding to change over following a plan

questions.question: What are the advantages of using Agile methodology over traditional Waterfall model?
keyPoints: Ability to handle changing requirements
Early and continuous delivery of valuable software
Better customer satisfaction through regular feedback
Improved risk management through iterative approach
Higher quality through continuous integration and testing

questions.question: Explain the roles of Product Owner and Scrum Master in Scrum framework.
keyPoints: Product Owner: manages Product Backlog, prioritizes features based on business value, represents stakeholder interests
Scrum Master: facilitates Scrum ceremonies, removes impediments, coaches team on Agile practices, serves as servant-leader

questions.question: What is a Sprint in Scrum? Describe the activities that occur during a Sprint.
keyPoints: Sprint is a time-boxed iteration (2-4 weeks)
Sprint Planning: plan work for the Sprint
Daily Standup: daily sync meeting
Sprint Review: demonstrate increment to stakeholders
Sprint Retrospective: team reflects and identifies improvements

questions.question: Explain the concept of Kanban and its key principles.
keyPoints: Visual workflow management method
Use of Kanban board with columns (To Do, In Progress, Done)
WIP (Work In Progress) limits to prevent overloading
Continuous delivery and flow optimization
No mandatory roles or time-boxes unlike Scrum

questions.question: What are the disadvantages of Traditional Project Management?
keyPoints: Inflexibility to changing requirements
Late delivery of value to customer
High risk due to late testing
Poor adaptability to market changes
Limited customer collaboration leads to dissatisfaction

questions.question: Compare and contrast Traditional (Waterfall) and Agile (Scrum) project management methodologies. Provide a detailed analysis of their differences in terms of requirements, planning, execution, and delivery.
keyPoints: Requirements: Waterfall - fixed upfront; Scrum - evolving backlog
Planning: Waterfall - comprehensive upfront; Scrum - iterative sprint planning
Execution: Waterfall - sequential phases; Scrum - cross-functional sprints
Delivery: Waterfall - one-time delivery; Scrum - incremental deliveries
Documentation: Waterfall - extensive; Scrum - minimal just-in-time
Customer Involvement: Waterfall - limited; Scrum - continuous collaboration
Risk Management: Waterfall - upfront analysis; Scrum - iterative adaptation
Team Structure: Waterfall - hierarchical; Scrum - self-organizing teams
Use Cases: Waterfall - stable requirements; Scrum - uncertain/volatile requirements

questions.question: Describe the Scrum framework in detail. Explain all Scrum events (ceremonies) and artifacts.
keyPoints: Scrum Framework: iterative and incremental approach
Scrum Roles: Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team
Sprint: time-boxed iteration (2-4 weeks)
Sprint Planning: plan sprint backlog
Daily Scrum: 15-min daily sync
Sprint Review: demonstrate increment to stakeholders
Sprint Retrospective: team improvement
Product Backlog: prioritized list of requirements
Sprint Backlog: items selected for current sprint
Increment: sum of all completed items meeting Definition of Done

questions.question: Explain the Agile Manifesto values and principles. How do they influence modern software development practices?
keyPoints: Four Values: individuals over processes, working software over docs, collaboration over contracts, change over plan

12 Principles: customer satisfaction through early delivery, welcome changing requirements, frequent delivery, business and developers work together daily, build projects around motivated individuals, face-to-face conversation, working software is primary measure, sustainable development pace, continuous attention to technical excellence, simplicity, self-organizing teams, regular adaptation to changing circumstances
Influence: led to DevOps, CI/CD, test-driven development, continuous feedback loops, customer-centric development

questions.question: What is Kanban? Explain its core principles and how it differs from Scrum.
keyPoints: Kanban: visual workflow management method developed by Toyota
Core Principles: visualize work, limit WIP, manage flow, make policies explicit, implement feedback loops, improve collaboratively
Kanban Board: columns representing workflow stages
WIP Limits: prevent overloading, improve throughput
Differences from Scrum: no fixed roles, no time-boxed iterations, continuous delivery, more flexible, can be applied to existing teams easily
Similarities: both are Agile, both limit WIP, both emphasize continuous improvement
